serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single chicken liver weighs approximately 44 grams and is commonly used as one serving in stir-fries, grilled dishes, or pâté preparations.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, liver, all classes, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05130); supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database (cr_id F11010029)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are taken from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chicken liver, raw (cr_id F11010029), which is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat (1.56g), monounsaturated fat (1.24g), polyunsaturated fat (0.97g), trans fat (0.0g), and all micronutrients not reported by HPB are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Chicken, liver, all classes, raw (NDB No. 05130 / FDC ID 172544), which is an approved secondary global source. Both sources are on the approved source list per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. Values from both sources are in close agreement for all shared fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chicken liver, raw contains only 0.73g carbohydrate per 100g. Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology and Project Veritas v2 protocol, glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. No GI value is assigned. gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per the zero/low-carbohydrate food rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (71mg/100g) with favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (230mg K vs 71mg Na); saturated fat approximately 1.56g/100g is well below the 5g threshold.
hypertension.guidance: Raw chicken liver is suitable for hypertensive individuals in its unprocessed form; avoid high-sodium marinades or sauces during preparation, and limit frequency to once weekly given the very high cholesterol content (345mg/100g) which is relevant for those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.73g/100g) with high protein (16.92g/100g) results in minimal glycaemic impact; exceptionally high folate (578mcg DFE/100g) and vitamin B12 (16.58mcg/100g) support metabolic health in diabetic patients.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken liver is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high-quality protein; limit to once weekly due to very high cholesterol (345mg/100g) and avoid high-sugar glazes or sauces during cooking.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Exceptionally high dietary cholesterol (345mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is moderate at 1.56g/100g; however, the food is rich in cardioprotective nutrients including folate, vitamin B12, and iron.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit chicken liver to no more than once per fortnight due to its very high cholesterol content (345mg/100g); when consumed, prepare by grilling or pan-frying with minimal oil and avoid high-sodium or high-fat accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken liver, raw is the uncooked organ meat from the liver of a domestic chicken (Gallus gallus domesticus). It displays a dark reddish-brown colour with a smooth, slightly firm texture and visible large vessels. Raw chicken liver has a rich, metallic, and intensely savoury aroma characteristic of organ meats. It is used globally in a wide range of culinary preparations including stir-fries, pâté, mousse, grilled skewers, and braised dishes. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, chicken liver is commonly used in hawker dishes such as char kway teow, satay, and congee. Nutritionally, chicken liver is one of the most nutrient-dense foods available: it provides 16.92g of high-quality complete protein per 100g, 4.83g of total fat with only 1.56g saturated fat, and a negligible 0.73g of carbohydrate. It is exceptionally rich in vitamin A (3296mcg RAE/100g — well above the adult RDA), vitamin B12 (16.58mcg/100g), folate (578mcg DFE/100g), iron (8.99mg/100g), and selenium (54.6mcg/100g). Its very high cholesterol content (345mg/100g) is the primary clinical caution, making frequency limitation important for individuals with cardiovascular disease, dyslipidaemia, or combined hypertension. Sodium is low at 71mg/100g. The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Chicken liver is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How much iron is in chicken liver?
Is chicken liver good for anaemia?
Is chicken liver high in cholesterol?
Is chicken liver safe for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11010029, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. Fatty acid breakdown and additional micronutrients cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Chicken, liver, all classes, raw (FDC ID 172544), an approved secondary global source. Both sources are in close agreement for all shared fields.
notes: Sugar_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — chicken liver contains no free sugars). Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (naturally zero — organ meat contains no dietary fibre). Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172544). Saturated fat (1.56g), monounsaturated fat (1.24g), polyunsaturated fat (0.97g), trans fat (0.0g), vitamin C (17.9mg), vitamin A (3296mcg RAE), thiamine (0.29mg), riboflavin (1.77mg), niacin (9.73mg), vitamin B6 (0.76mg), vitamin B12 (16.58mcg), folate (578mcg DFE), vitamin D (0.5mcg), vitamin E (0.67mg), phosphorus (297mg), magnesium (19mg), zinc (2.67mg), selenium (54.6mcg), and water (76.0g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172544 (Chicken, liver, all classes, raw) to supplement HPB fields not reported. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.73g/100g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 protocol. The input record referenced vitamin C at 17mg/100g in health_suitability text; this is consistent with USDA SR Legacy value of 17.9mg/100g and has been retained. Cholesterol at 345mg/100g is exceptionally high and is the primary clinical caution for cardiovascular and dyslipidaemia management. Vitamin A content (3296mcg RAE/100g) exceeds the adult upper tolerable intake level if consumed in large quantities; frequency limitation is advised for pregnant women due to teratogenic risk at very high vitamin A intakes.
